speaker: interviewer
text: I know this is hard for you, but before we leave Auschwitz, talk about how they searched you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first searching really happened still in Chust before we boarded the train. Every woman was called in in a tent. I never, ever told this story to anybody, and not in, in the details I'm going to say it now. I don't know why I'm doing it. But it must, must be said. There was a tent. There was a midwife, a non-Jewish midwife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was Hungarian policemen, one or two. They were very tall. And the Hungarian police, they had a high black hat with feathers. We used to call them-- the featherman is coming! We're standing there. And there was a bench. And very fast, they screamed, lay her on the bench! Take off your panties! And spread your legs! And, and, and the woman came and examined me internally, if I hid something. And I was in such a shock because I didn't know that those kinds of examinations even exist. I was 16 and 1/2. I knew about sex. At least, I thought I knew a lot. I, I knew how to-- babies are born. But I had no idea that you can be examined like this. And she said, clean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I know after this, I was in such a shock. And after days, mother asked us once, were you searched, too? Because she knew that we are both virgins and, and to be examined. And we told her, yes. And we never, ever talked about it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And only recently when I see so much happening in life now, I started to, to think that this is how a woman feels who is raped. I was raped at that time, not by a man, by an old, ugly woman, anti-Semitic woman. Because the way she did it, the way she talked, and the-- those policemen standing around, it was the, the top of humiliation that anybody can imagine. It was even-- I wouldn't say it. And I wouldn't want to try, God forbid, say it's worse than rape because there were other people standing there. It's not two people. So in a way, it was very horrible. And then this was one of the things that I couldn't get out of my mind for many, many years. And until I saw my mother alive, I couldn't get out. And then another thing what I saw in Auschwitz, actually several things, one was that we were going to the train in Auschwitz. There was-- I heard a voice, sounded like a young girl, help me! Help me! Help me, please! Help me! Get me out of here. And I looked and searched and didn't know what it is. Finally, I saw little bars on the ground, maybe 12 inches above the ground. And it was like a cellar. And there were people. And this young girl was standing there inside, like animals in a cage, and screaming and crying for help. And I stood there and looked at her. And I wished with all my mind I could have helped. But there was nothing I could do. And I left her. I went-- I had to go with my group. And till today, I am sure that those people were taken to the gas chambers. And she knew it, too. And she never cried and screamed. And there was nothing anybody could do.

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] You said you wanted to talk about your last day in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the last day in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the last day in Auschwitz. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then when we arrived at the station, then we are all cleaned with clothes. We're supposed to go to the train. A commotion started. And we saw-- it wasn't my Blockalteste-- the other girls, kapos and Blockaltestes and SS. And they were arguing. And I was surprised that those girls were really arguing. They had the guts to argue with the SS. And they were arguing back and forth and back and forth. And finally, after the-- maybe an hour of arguments-- they were running around-- they let us go in the train. And the girls were, like, relieved that we are going-- the girls who were arguing for us. They didn't come with us. But when we boarded the train, and they closed the doors, we didn't move. And suddenly we  heard explosions, like bombing. And the sirens went off. And we heard explosions. And we saw that they are bombing Auschwitz. And somehow, later-- I went through a lot of bombings. But I was not afraid because I always felt that the Allies won't hurt me. I'm not the enemy. They will bomb only the Germans. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then when they stopped--

speaker: interviewer
text: If we can stop, the family is coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we can stop now, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, keep going. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the commotion was going on. And then it stopped, the bombing and everything. And we went-- we started to go. Somebody knew inside the train that-- what was going on. A few SS came and saw the group of us. They decided they had room in a gas chamber. And they needed people to fill it up. So they decided they wanted to take us. But these girls-- those girls, they knew that they were supposed to deliver us to a work assignment. If they would have given us out to the gas chambers, they would join us. So they did two, two jobs. They saved our lives and including their own, too. But they had a lot of guts to fight the SS. And they succeeded. And the bombing, what happened-- nobody bombed Auschwitz. We know it today. After the war, I found out that this was the night when inmates, who succeeded after months of collecting, piece by piece, explosives, they exploded two, two crematorias. They succeeded. There was a little rebellion. And two of the crematorias exploded. And some SS were killed. But I left Auschwitz, so I don't know what happened later. But after the war, I found out this, this is what the sirens were for. And this is what happened at that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you heard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I heard it after the war. And I know the date. It was the 4th of October, supposedly, yeah. And what I had the feeling about going away from Auschwitz, it was true. Because the other camp, we worked very hard. But the food was better. We had our single-bed bunk beds, only two layers, not three levels. And everything was different. But we had to work very hard. But there, I was also very lucky in a certain way. [DRINKS] I, I didn't-- we had to-- we made a Panzerfaust, was a, a anti-tank missile, a small one. Two girls worked at a machine. And then we started-- made, like-- for one machine, I was working in the welding department. One girl put up the, the, the pipe. And the, the other had to hold it-- a little piece of plastic, had seven-- I don't remember how many holes. But we had to push 11 times the pedal-- the foot pedal to weld the little pipe-- the little metal piece on the pipe. And they had holes. And the holes had to match perfectly. Otherwise, they had to take it back to another department to chisel it off. And it take ten times more times to take off one-- 20 times more than to make one. And the girls worked. And we made, like, 500 pieces a day. Then they pushed us. And they make 800. And they brought it up to a thousand. And when the SS saw-- and the foremens were older German civilians. And when they saw that we can make a thousand-- and one day, they announced if you make 1,500 a day, you will get a bonus. A bonus, some extra food, was very, very tempting. By that time-- this was quite-- after several weeks that we were-- we were there. And this was-- already I learned to do some sabotage. There were two ways. One way was when I did welding the-- when I was welding, I moved a little bit the part, the little metal part, so they didn't cover completely the holes. And then it had to go back. And I will-- with this, I could slow down the, the process. And I felt it's-- now, I was very careful that no-- if the foreman was there or SS, then I didn't do it. And the other thing was I got-- I organized myself a piece of chalk because the people-- the men who brought the pipes and the pieces to the machine, every time they brought a batch, they made a chalk mark. And every chalk mark meant how many pieces we made. And then I organized those-- the, the chalk. When we had already several lines-- even my partner didn't know it-- I always added an extra line. So even if I, I accumulated a thousand pieces I made, I actually made only 700 with my partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started to walk around the girls. And when they announced the bonus, I said, girls, what are you working so much? Don't you want to go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they looked at me with the sad eyes. They didn't say anything. They didn't argue with me. But they didn't-- but they worked. They wanted a bonus. And then one day, the bonus came. And only-- and I didn't get one, of course, because I had never made 1,500 pieces. And those girls who made it got a herring, a whole herring. Now, to tell you that I didn't care would be a lie. I did care. And I said, I could have had it, too. A whole herring, and I missed it. But they never gave another bonus. And when I saw this was only this one time, then I realized I did the right thing. And I felt very good about it. And one day from the Wehrmacht, the high officer came. And he went around the, the, the factory. And he stand-- we had a conveying belt. And he was standing at the head of it. And he was looking. And I worked very diligently and very carefully. And I heard him saying to the foreman-- he didn't know who understands. And I understood perfectly German. And so he said, somebody is sabotaging in this factory. Because some of the-- of the missiles, they come. And they go to the wrong direction. And because one missile is supposed to go to the tank and to destroy it. And they go in a different position, they don't-- And when I heard this, I felt my, my heart jumping. I said, I am one of those. And I'm not the only one because I was-- there were many departments. I am not the only one. A lot of us are doing that. And I felt that I am doing the right thing. And then, one night-- and we had to-- the hardest thing was to work at night from 6:00 in the evening, 6:00 in the morning. Because at night, you didn't receive any food. You had an hour, midnight, an hour break. But what happened, daytime you went to work. Lunchtime, you had an hour break. You got-- the vegetable soup was much better and, and, and thicker and even had tiny pieces of meat in it. So we received this. And then when we went home, we received the evening meal, the bread. You know, I never talked about it, piece of bread, a thin slice of salami, and a little square of margarine. This was the ration. It was bigger and a little bit better than in Auschwitz. And if we succeeded to, to save a little piece of bread, we kept it for breakfast. But if you work at night, you came home on empty stomach, you went to sleep-- home-- you came back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --camp. You went to sleep. Then they woke you up lunchtime to have the soup. So you got up, you had the soup, you went back to sleep. Then they woke you up to get your evening meal and to, to wash up and go to work. And you were so hungry you almost ate all the bread. And then the whole night, you had to work 12 hours physically and no food. And one day at night, I think I fell asleep. A sudden, I heard somebody tapping me. And I opened my eyes. And the foreman was standing there. And I got so scared. Probably I was so scared that I lost my blood from my face. And I think he felt sorry for me. Because I was 17 at the time, but I looked like 12. I was very skinny, my little  hair. And he looked at me and said, listen, you don't work like the other girls work. I will put you on day shift only. But if you won't produce a thousand pieces a day, I will report you to the SS. And my god, nothing could be better at that moment in my life. And I promised him, I said, thank you, thank you. And I will work hard. And I will do it. And this was maybe another link in the chain of my survival that I didn't have to work any more at night. So at night I could sleep. And that was the only time that you didn't feel the hunger, when you were sleeping. So this was a blessing for me. And then the winter came. Our shoes get-- got holes in them. And I got a cold. And we had a little infirmary there. So somebody was sick, after work you could go. And there was a doctor, a Hungarian Jew. The only instrument he had was a thermometer. And I felt some pain in my left shoulder. And I didn't want to go to the doctor. [DRINKS] Because if you got sick there, sometimes you had to-- they took you away to another camp. And you never came back. And you don't know what happened. It was safer to stay there. And the girls urged me. One day, I was coughing. And I didn't feel well. And I felt feverish. So they forced me, and I went to the doctor. The doctor was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to him. He tapped on my lung. He said, your-- I think your, your left shoulder is a little bit affected. But your fever is not too high. Go back to work. And if you feel-- don't feel better, come back again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't feel better. And after a few days, I came back again. He said, I will put you in the infirmary. It was a little-- one room, one big room with 20 beds, like a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was very scared. I said, but won't you send me away? I, I don't want to go away. I want to stay with my friends, the new friends I made.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, oh, no, my child. Don't worry. I won't send-- I will take care of you. He must be-- must have been in his 40s because over 50, you didn't have too many people there. But for me he, was an older man, a very kind man. And then he put me in the infirmary. And this was another-- like a vacation. I could sleep there and didn't have to get up 4 o'clock in the morning to go to work. I-- the food was the same. And I was in a warm room and in a bed. And there were-- at this camp, we had 200 non-Jewish women from all over Europe-- Poland, Germany, France, Holland, whatever-- non-Jewish women who were all political prisoners, or they were criminals, and they were taken out from jails and put in labor-- forced labor camps. And I had several people. There was a group of, of Polish women who talk all day long Polish. And every second was [SPEAKING POLISH] that I felt I'm getting crazy. [LAUGHTER] But I didn't dare to say anything. And one-- next to me was a girl, a young girl about 20 years old. And she had syphilis, not more, not less. And I knew about syphilis. But I didn't know exactly what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she showed me how she looked, her body. It was horrible. She was all broken out. And, and I, I said, how did you get it? She said, there was a young man who-- and she was a dumb girl, really. I mean, not because she had syphilis, but she was dumb. And she had such a funny face. Maybe she-- I-- she wasn't normal. I said, what did you do? How did you get it? She said a, a man came to her, and he  promised he will marry her. And she slept with him. And he had syphilis and infected her. And then she was taken away one day and never heard from her again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a young woman came in. And she had an abscess on her leg with, with pus, and a lot. She was about a 28-year-old woman. And she was very happy because she had two children. And she left her two children with non-Jewish families. And she was full with hope that she will find them after the war. But she was very sick. And the doctor looked at her and couldn't do anything. He said, there is one thing I could try. If you will be good, I can put you on under hypnosis. And I have a little pocket knife. That's all what I can do, try to open it. And she was ready. She said, anything, just save my life. And he tried to hypnotize her, to put her under hypnosis, put out-- took out a, a, a stone with a little string. And we watched it. And she fell asleep. None of us fell asleep, just her. And then he took this little knife and put it in a fire. And he operated on her. And we heard her moaning. But he went through. And we gave her a piece of our undershirt. We, we took some bandages to make her. He bandaged her. He woke her up. And she says she doesn't remember anything. And then he came in  a few days to change her bandage. And she recuperated. And she left. And I was there-- I don't remember for how long. But one day, a very-- a miracle happened to me, a strange thing happened to me. This was a big room with 20 people. And, and the nurse-- the chart nurse was a girl from Ukraine, a very anti-Semitic woman. She always tried to liquidate-- to send out the Jewish patients and to get in non-Jewish. Because it was like a, a little rest there. It was good to be there, as long as they didn't send you away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day-- and in the front, was a door that you came in. And there was two-- like a hole. And there were two benches. And when somebody came to visit, you could go out. If you were not infectious, then you could go out there and meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other side was the little room that the doctor used to see the, the patients. But when we were in there, he used to come in to check us. And if you didn't have fever, they send you out. So this was the only thing how they checked us. And one day, I went-- I wanted to go to the bathroom. We, we had to go through this hallway. And there were lying a pair of socks, gray woolen socks, brand new, right from a store. And I had just finished not only my, my socks, but almost my shoes. I put paper in my shoes for the winter because I didn't have anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were those socks. And nobody was around me. And I looked at them. And I thought, who do they belong to? Because if it belongs to somebody, I wouldn't touch them. I am not going to touch them. But I said, if I don't touch them, whoever will come out after me will take them. And I need them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was debating with myself, maybe for a minute, maybe for 10. I don't know. Finally, I took the socks, hide them under my dress, and I went back to my bed. [DRINKS] And I came to my bed and put them under my mattress. We had mattresses there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was lying in bed. And suddenly, the nurse came in, shouting and screaming, I had a pair of gray socks! Who took them? And I said, oh, my, my god, am I in trouble. And she was screaming and shouting, where are her socks? I am going to search, and god help the person who had them. I would have given it back gladly. But I knew if I give it back, she will beat me up again-- anyway. So I was laying on my socks-- on her socks, praying. And suddenly, she went out of the room for a few minutes. She was outside. I didn't hear a door opening, the other door. I didn't hear her talking to anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she walked in. And she was holding the gray socks. And she said, I found my socks. And the matter was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was like numb. I said, what happened here? So when the lights went out that night, I put my hand under my mattress. And the socks were there. I had my socks, and she had her socks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until today, I cannot figure out how it happened. It only was a miracle that another pair of socks walked in there, and she found them. Because she would have beaten me up real badly if she wouldn't have found her socks. And this is my-- and it happened around wintertime, like around-- probably during Hanukkah. So I had my  own Hanukkah miracle. And I left the infirmary. I put on my socks. It was January when I left. And I-- those socks I kept until the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that camp again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taucha. I really didn't mention the name. Taucha was a little village near, near Leipzig. Leipzig was not far away. And we walked to the-- to the-- to work every day. When I walked-- we walked, not too many SSes were around us. And we walked in the streets. And usually, the streets were dark, because in the wintertime, 6:00 in the morning, 6:00 in the evening was always dark. And every day, every time I went there, this crossed my mind. I could just step out on-- into the bushes and hide. Oh, they will discover that I am missing. But it was easy-- would have been so easy to walk away. But the problem was, I didn't have where to go. They would recognize me right away. And then I would pay with my life. So nobody tried to escape. But escape itself would have been easy. It just wasn't easy because you have to go somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at the very end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the war end for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The end of the war-- we knew that the war is going to end. We always heard news. If they were good, we believed them. If they were not good, we called them German propaganda. Of course, we wanted always to believe that the war will be-- go to an end, and we will survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in April, we knew already that the Allies are closing in. But we didn't  know if it would last a month, two, five, six. We didn't know how, how long. So one day, we were ready to go to work. So we were standing outside, the day shift, 6 o'clock in the morning-- no, before 6:00. And-- but we didn't go to work. They let us stand there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the night shift who came back, they didn't let them to go inside the barracks. And they were standing. And then they said, we are going away. And somehow-- we had blankets in this camp already. We run back to the barracks and grabbed our blanket. Everything else was on us what we had, the, the dress and the coat. And then we started on a march. And we were marching-- in the literature of the Holocaust, it's called a 'death march,' because a lot of people lost their lives on those marches the last weeks of the war. Or even not the last weeks-- I think my brother lost his life because he was on a march in February. In February in Europe, to march without shoes in the cold, a lot of people lost their lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went on a march. It lasted for three weeks. And many things happened on those march-- on this march. But the end of the march was the war ended. And I got back my freedom. So we marched for three weeks. And first, it was strange places. And I saw the signs of the villages. And we walked through villages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people digged out potatoes, frozen potatoes there. Because we-- the first week, we received about twice a little piece of bread; the second week, once; and the third week, nothing. And this time, the hunger was threatening us, really, with, with, with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dying of hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with death. And people were eating whatever they could find, peels of, of potato. I tried it, too. But some, I couldn't swallow it. And then finally, we ended up in a village. And when we ended up in this village-- not in a village, in a forest. And the SS were so frantic already. And what I saw in the village that gave me another hope was that I saw signs on the walls, [SPEAKING GERMAN], 'We will not surrender.' And when I saw this, I said, I never heard it while I was free that Hitler would say we will not-- so this is a sign the war is going to end. And, and my heart jumped from joy. But still the fear was there. The SS were around. And we didn't know what they might do. And they really did kill from the men a lot of people at the last minute when they were-- they were already running away. And they still used their machine guns. There was a father who kept his little 12-year-old son for a whole year, saving him. And he was the only child that we had. And he was guarding him like a father his own child. And then after the war, I heard from another man I met in Budapest that the SS, when they shot people, he shot the father and the son, the last--

speaker: interviewer
text: Moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: --moment of the war. So it, it-- when I remember them, it breaks my heart that for a whole year he tried so hard. And, and, and his destiny was to die anyway. And many things happened on those march. One thing that is very interesting for me is we were in a village. And we looked really dirty and hungry. And the rain was falling down. So we had our blankets on our head. And they were all wet. But we still used them. And I looked down. And suddenly, I saw a familiar face. And I didn't believe my eyes. It was a girl from Chust, a non-Jewish girl. She actually came to live in Chust during the war already. She wasn't originally born there. And her name was Magda. And I looked at her. And she saw the group. If she would have seen me, she wouldn't know who I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at her. And I was so hungry. And I said, shall I go to ask her? Maybe she has a piece-- shall I go, or shall I not go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were standing there for some time. And I was looking at her and thinking. And I couldn't make up my mind. And believe it or not, I did not go up to her. I didn't want her to see me in my condition. Because she was an anti-Semite in Chust already. But my heart already said, she had to run away. Because we knew that Chust was already liberated. And if she was there in a German village-- I didn't know even the name of the village. I still don't know. If she was there, she had to run away. So she had a little taste of war. And I couldn't make myself-- in spite of my hunger, I couldn't make myself to go up to her and ask for a-- for a-- maybe, maybe she had. And maybe she would have said she doesn't. But I didn't do it. So I will never know. And then we went to this forest. And they let us stay there. They didn't bother us anymore. But the hunger was so unbearable that people start to go to the villages to look for food. And once a girl disappeared. And another time, two girls were shot. And they came back, and then one girl died. While we were watching, she bled, bled to death. And there was nothing we could do, a young girl. She was shot in the shin. Because-- and the doctor came. We found the doctor. But there was nothing he could do because he didn't have anything. And then the third day, after all this, what was so-- I picked up myself. And I said to my group of girls with  whom I was together, I said, I'm going to look for food. And they looked at me, you are crazy. You saw what happened. I said, maybe it will be easier to die from a bullet than from hunger. We didn't know how long it will last. And I said, I'm going. And nobody would come. I said, I'm going on my own. And then one girl stood up. [DRINKS] I only know that her name was Irene. She was twice my size and probably twice my age, and maybe not. But she looked older. And she said to me, Edith, I will go with you. And we picked ourselves, and we went. And we walked for a long time. And nobody bothered us. We saw SS people. And they were so worried for themselves how to run away from the Russians that they didn't pay attention to us. This was already the third day that we've had there. And then we walked and walked. And finally, we reached a house. And Irene spoke only Hungarian. And, you know, Hungarian can use, use only in Hungary. So I was the speaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knocked on the door. A woman opened the door. And I told her who I am. I'm from concentration camp. And I told her, you know, I am-- we are very, very hungry. Please give us a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: She looked at us, said, I don't have any. And she locked the door. And she wouldn't give us a piece of bread. And we went to next house and next house. And, and we almost gave up. And they wouldn't give us, those civilians, a piece of bread. And then in one door, a young girl opened the door. And she was probably my age. And I gave her the same story. And she again said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], we don't have any. But I saw a dish in the corner. And in those days, they didn't go to the stores to buy food for pets. They gave leftovers. And she want to close the door. I put my foot in the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, wait a minute. Can I have this food that's there in the, the corner? She said, this is for the dog. I said, I don't mind. Can I have it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was smiling, like how stupid I am I want the leftovers. And she gave us the dish of food. And the two of us, we shared this dish of food. It was the best meal we had since we arrived in Auschwitz. And we got a little bit of energy and a little bit of hope. I gave her back the dish. I thanked her. And we continued.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it became dark. And Irene said, Edith, get-- let's go back to, to the forest. I said, Irene, I don't know how to go back. We never looked back where we come. We never find-- and then, why should I go back? Let's try to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt now we can-- we can-- we can hide. Now I will risk. I am not going back to the group. There is nothing to go back to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I started to ask places to hide. And again, the answer was no. And finally, I came to one house. And I gave her a whole speech for the-- for the woman. She said, OK, why don't you go there. And she pointed to me. [POINTS] And I went where she pointed. It was a little house and a door. I opened the door and went in. It had a roof, a window, and a floor. So we're out of the rain. And it had a huge kettle of hot water. We went inside. We closed the door. We took off our clothes. And we washed ourselves without soap. We didn't have any soap. We washed ourselves with warm water. And I said, now, Irene, we can relax. I'm not going anywhere. We didn't have any food, but we didn't care. We got up the next morning. We slept on the cement floor. It's better than on the wet ground. And we got up the next morning. We looked out the window.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was so much commotion outside, all the German, SS, and regular soldiers, the tearing of the insignias, changing their clothes, going away. And we felt it must end soon. But still, the question was, when?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then suddenly, someone knocked at the door. They opened the door. And a tall man was standing there. And he had on green overall. And he-- in broken German, he asked us who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I answered him. And, and I told him who we are. And I said, who are you? And he had-- I'm  a French POW. And I'm working here in this farm.
